name: Virata
description: Virata was the king of the Matsya Kingdom, a prominent figure in the epic Mahabharata. He is renowned for his righteousness, hospitality, and bravery. During the Pandavas' thirteenth year of exile, which had to be spent incognito, they sought refuge in Virata's kingdom. Yudhishthira, Bhima, Arjuna, Nakula, and Sahadeva, along with Draupadi, disguised themselves and served in Virata's court under various pseudonyms. Virata unknowingly provided shelter and employment to the Pandavas, treating them with respect and generosity. He appointed Yudhishthira as his advisor, Bhima as his cook and wrestler, Arjuna as a dance instructor, Nakula as a horse caretaker, Sahadeva as a cattle caretaker, and Draupadi as a maid to his queen, Sudeshna. The Pandavas maintained their anonymity throughout this period, successfully completing their exile. Later, when the Pandavas revealed their true identities, Virata was overwhelmed with joy and offered his daughter, Uttara, in marriage to Arjuna's son, Abhimanyu, solidifying a strong alliance. Virata fought valiantly on the Pandava side in the Kurukshetra War and was ultimately slain by Drona. His role in providing sanctuary to the Pandavas during their crucial period of exile and his unwavering support for dharma mark him as a significant and honorable character in the Mahabharata, embodying the virtues of a just and compassionate ruler.
subtitle: Righteous King, Protector of the Pandavas
achievements: Provided refuge to the Pandavas during their exile
Maintained a just and prosperous kingdom
Offered his daughter in marriage to Abhimanyu
Fought bravely in the Kurukshetra War
Embodied the virtues of a righteous king

enemies.name: Drona (slain by him in the Kurukshetra war)

keyMyths.title: Sheltering the Pandavas
keyMyths.description: During their exile, the Pandavas sought refuge in Virata's kingdom, disguised as commoners. Virata unknowingly provided them with shelter and employment, showcasing his hospitality and righteousness.

keyMyths.title: Uttara's Marriage
keyMyths.description: After the Pandavas revealed their identities, Virata offered his daughter, Uttara, in marriage to Abhimanyu, Arjuna's son. This alliance strengthened the Pandava's position and ensured Virata's continued support.

keyMyths.title: Death in Kurukshetra
keyMyths.description: Virata fought valiantly on the Pandava side in the Kurukshetra War, displaying his bravery and loyalty. He was ultimately killed by Drona, sacrificing himself for the cause of dharma.

sources.author: John Dowson
sources.title: A Classical Dictionary of Hindu Mythology & Religion
sources.description: Provides detailed entries on characters and events from Hindu epics, including information on Virata and the Matsya Kingdom.

sources.author: Wendy Doniger
sources.title: The Hindus: An Alternative History
sources.description: Offers a nuanced perspective on Hindu mythology, exploring the cultural and historical context of figures like Virata.

associations.name: Dharma
associations.significance: Virata embodies righteousness and adherence to dharma, offering refuge to the Pandavas in their time of need.

associations.name: Matsya Kingdom
associations.significance: As the king, Virata represents stability, prosperity, and just rule over his kingdom.

associations.name: Refuge
associations.significance: Virata's willingness to shelter the Pandavas highlights his commitment to hospitality and protecting those in need.

associations.name: Warrior
associations.significance: Virata is a skilled warrior, demonstrating courage and strength in battle, particularly during the Kurukshetra War.

associations.name: Allegiance
associations.significance: His allegiance to the Pandavas, even after their identities are revealed, showcases his unwavering loyalty.

associations.name: Uttara
associations.significance: Virata's role as a father, particularly to Uttara, is significant as it connects his lineage to Abhimanyu and the future of the Kuru dynasty.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Virata, Alcinous offers hospitality and aid to a hero in need (Odysseus), providing refuge and assistance.
Hrothgar, like Virata, is a strong king who provides shelter and protection to heroes, as seen with Beowulf.
Similar to Virata, Conchobar is a noble king known for his just rule and the protection he offers to his people and allies.

worshipPractices.name: Temple Offerings
worshipPractices.description: While Virata is not directly worshipped, offerings may be made at temples dedicated to Vishnu or other deities to honor virtuous kings and protectors like him. Recitations of the Mahabharata also serve to commemorate his virtues.

cultural.modernLegacy: Virata's character continues to be celebrated in dramatic performances, literature, and art, serving as a symbol of righteous kingship and selfless hospitality in Indian culture. His story is often invoked to exemplify the importance of dharma and protecting those in need.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Reign
extendedContent.content: Virata was the ruler of the Matsya Kingdom, a region located in present-day Rajasthan, India. Details about his early life are scarce, but he is depicted as a just and capable ruler, ensuring prosperity and stability for his people. His reign was characterized by peace and adherence to dharma, making him a respected and beloved king. The Matsya Kingdom was known for its agricultural wealth and its skilled warriors, contributing to Virata's strength and influence in the region. He maintained cordial relations with neighboring kingdoms, fostering an environment of cooperation and mutual respect. Virata's court was renowned for its wisdom and justice, attracting scholars and wise men from various parts of the country. He was a patron of the arts and sciences, promoting cultural development and intellectual growth within his kingdom. His commitment to dharma extended to all aspects of his governance, ensuring fairness and equity for all his subjects.

extendedContent.title: The Pandavas' Incognito Exile
extendedContent.content: The most significant episode in Virata's life is his role in sheltering the Pandavas during their thirteenth year of exile. As per the terms of their defeat in a game of dice, the Pandavas had to spend one year incognito, without being recognized. They chose to seek refuge in Virata's kingdom, disguising themselves and serving in his court under assumed identities. Yudhishthira became Kanka, a dice player and advisor; Bhima worked as a cook named Vallabha; Arjuna transformed into Brihannala, a dance teacher; Nakula took on the role of Granthika, a horse keeper; and Sahadeva became Tantipala, a cattle herder. Draupadi, disguised as Sairandhri, a lady-in-waiting, served Queen Sudeshna. Virata, unaware of their true identities, welcomed them all, providing them with employment and protection. His hospitality and trust in these individuals proved crucial to the Pandavas' successful completion of their exile. During this time, Bhima protected the kingdom by defeating Keechaka, the commander of the Matsya army, who lusted after Draupadi. This act further solidified Virata's respect for the disguised Pandavas.

extendedContent.title: Role in the Kurukshetra War and Legacy
extendedContent.content: Upon the revelation of the Pandavas' true identities, Virata was overjoyed and offered his daughter, Uttara, in marriage to Arjuna's son, Abhimanyu. This alliance strengthened the bond between the Matsya Kingdom and the Pandavas, solidifying Virata's support for their cause in the Kurukshetra War. Virata actively participated in the war, fighting valiantly on the Pandava side. He demonstrated his bravery and skill as a warrior, contributing to their eventual victory. Although he faced numerous challenges and losses during the war, Virata remained steadfast in his commitment to dharma and his allegiance to the Pandavas. Virata's legacy extends beyond his role in the Mahabharata. He is remembered as a righteous and compassionate king who exemplified the virtues of hospitality, loyalty, and courage. His story serves as an inspiration for leaders and individuals alike, highlighting the importance of upholding dharma and protecting those in need. His name is synonymous with just rule and unwavering support for the righteous cause.

shortDescription: King of Matsya, renowned for his righteousness and hospitality. He sheltered the Pandavas during their incognito exile, playing a key role in the Mahabharata.
